title: The Return of the Charrette
subtitle: What the architecture studio can teach academia in the era of AI
text:
In one of my recent courses, I was asked - for the first time - to add an "AI policy" to the syllabus. Here's what I came up with:

AI Policy

Whenever you think about using AI, replace the word AI with computer. If the sentence suddenly sounds outdated, go ahead and use AI. If it still sounds exciting, please report your discovery to the instructor immediately.

I hoped the lone student who actually read the syllabus that far would at least smile. But it was not only a joke. When I entered architecture school in the early 2000s, I was not allowed to use a computer. For the first two years of our five-year B.Arch program, the Foundations director, a Soviet-trained Russian immigrant who seemed ancient to us at the time - he was probably 40 - demanded "3,000 drafting hours" before we could touch digital tools. And so we drafted and drew and sketched until the residue of 6B pencils was embedded in our fingertips. But I could not help myself. It was the early Internet, and everywhere I looked there were incredible images of digital architectural craft: CAD drawings, 3D renderings, CAM models, speculative forms, and representations that seemed to arrive from the future. Late at night, I started running a shadow version of every studio project. There was the official project, drawn with pencils and T-squares, and there was the other one, never meant to see daylight, built in SketchUp, Maya, 3ds Max, AutoCAD, and whatever else I could get my hands on. Slowly, the shadow project started leaking into the official one: a rendering here, a 3D diagram there. Some faculty noticed. Then people started offering me freelance work. By the end of the first year, I had my first part-time architecture job. By my third year, I was working full time. Was I right? Was my professor right?

The Death of Homework

There are many faces to the current crisis in academia: politics, relevance, a brutal job market, and now the almighty rise of AI. The concerns of my Foundations professor suddenly echo in the worries I hear from colleagues across disciplines. With AI, many of our traditional forms of academic evaluation no longer work the way we pretend they do. The take-home essay, the polished research paper, the weekly response, the coding assignment, the design brief - all of them were built on a fragile assumption that the final submitted artifact is evidence of thinking. AI has broken that assumption. Academic work has always been a proxy for learning, the same way a rendering is a representation of a building, not the building itself. Students have always found ways to exploit the gap between the proxy and the goal, through the creative practice we usually call cheating. So when the first versions of ChatGPT appeared during the pandemic, academia reached for the familiar label to describe a new threat: if you did not write it, it is not your work. Period. But the problem cannot be reduced to cheating. The deeper issue with the way AI divorces the artifact from the process is that artifacts are no longer reliable evidence of learning. In the past, most assignments were indirect evidence of knowledge and skill. We looked at the paper and inferred the process. We looked at the answer and inferred the struggle. We looked at the project and inferred intellectual ownership. This inference is no longer reliable. In a last-ditch effort, some educators want to bring back handwritten exams, closed classrooms, and supervised tests. I understand the impulse. An in-class exam solves one problem: it creates a room where students cannot ask AI to produce the answer for them. It can show whether they can retrieve, compress, and reproduce something they studied at home. That is not nothing. But it is not enough. A pen-and-paper test measures a very narrow slice of student capability: recall, composure, speed, memorization, and the ability to extract digested knowledge on command. It does not show proof of effort over time. It does not show ownership. It does not show whether a student can build with an idea, revise it, apply it to a messy situation, explain a tradeoff, recover from failure, or make a judgment when the answer is not already sitting in the back of the book.

The Return of the Charrette

What if the goal of education is not only to retain knowledge, but to develop good judgment? What if the goal is not to recite a prepared response, but to own it? The problem with take-home work in the age of AI is not that students work outside class. They should work outside class. They should build projects, write, design, research, experiment, fail, revise, and develop ideas over time. Serious work needs duration, privacy, wandering, obsession. The problem is work that arrives in class as a finished artifact with no context around it: polished, but not understood; submitted, but not owned. This is where academic education should learn from the architecture studio. Studio is the spine of architectural education. Students take one almost every term, sometimes ten different studios before graduation. Each studio can meet for half a day, twice a week, and everything else in the curriculum - history, theory, structures, representation, technology - is supposed to feed the project on the wall. The dreaded charrette - French for "cart" or "chariot" - is usually traced to the Ecole des Beaux-Arts, where a cart was pushed through the studios to collect the drawings of exhausted students. The charrette is the concentrated burst inside the studio: making, pinning up, explaining, getting feedback, revising, and trying again. Studio is the marathon. Charrette is the sprint. In architecture education, students are rarely evaluated only by what they eventually submit. Even a great drawing, a perfect physical model, or a shiny 3D rendering is not enough. The student has to stand with the work, present it, explain it, defend it, respond to critique, and revise it. The assessment is not only of the object. It is of the student's relationship to the object. And this happens dozens of times during the term: in pin-ups, desk crits, impromptu reviews, and those strange moments when a guest suddenly appears and everyone has to explain their work on the spot. Demonstrating ownership is much harder to fake. Anyone who has taught in a studio, seminar, critique, or discussion-based classroom knows that within five minutes, it is often clear whether a student owns the work or has merely assembled it. Students who own their work can move around inside it. They can explain decisions, respond to unexpected questions, name doubts, and connect the project to readings, precedents, constraints, and earlier versions. They have context and agency. Students who do not own the work usually cannot. They might repeat the surface, but they cannot inhabit the structure. Every architectural firm in the world has shelves filled with reference material, precedent studies, details, images, and design examples. When those references are inherited into a project, they are not simply copied, and the designer is not "cheating" their way through the commission. But if they are only copying, if they do not understand what they borrowed or why, it becomes apparent in a heartbeat: here is a wannabe Mario Botta, but the masonry is wrong. Here is a copycat Alvaro Siza, but the proportions are off.

Demo or Die, Again

When I joined the MIT Media Lab around a decade ago, the "demo or die" provocation, which emerged as a reaction to academia's "publish or perish" culture, was setting the tone across the building. Instead of only imagining a possible future, go ahead and build a piece of it. Put it in a room. Let people touch it, question it, misunderstand it, break it, and ask why it matters. More than half of our lab space is designated for these demos. This is inefficient, almost irresponsible, and certainly expensive. But it is also the most important part of the lab. The demo space is a 24/7 workshop, studio, charrette, classroom, stage, and testing ground; a beehive of creativity in which objects serve as catalysts for conversation. The real point of the demo area is not to show finished shiny objects, but to create the live encounter around them. Sometimes a mayor, minister, company executive, or head of state walks in and points at a half-baked tangle of wires, sensors, and noisy actuators and asks: What is this thing? Why does it matter? What problem does it solve? Why did you build it this way? And then a student - who sometimes arrived in the States a few weeks before, still struggling with jet lag and language - has to answer and demonstrate ownership. The demo space does not replace traditional instruction at MIT. Students still go to classes, take exams, and publish papers. But the good students in our group spend the vast majority of their time and effort in that room. They physically stand there and build their projects in real time, turning inherited knowledge into something they can test, explain, defend, and improve. Courses serve as a foundational framework, but the demo space is where the work becomes work. Demo culture and the charrette are cousins. Both insist that knowledge has to stand up, not as performance for its own sake, but as proof of ownership and a demonstration of judgment. A student in a demo space is not only building something for others to review. They are also learning to communicate the thing, locate its weakness, defend its choices, absorb critique, and return to the project with sharper judgment. In this moment of knowledge abundance, the same logic should travel beyond architecture studios and demo spaces. A history student might submit a paper, but also present a source map, a timeline, and an oral defense. A computer science student might walk through code architecture, debugging decisions, and tradeoffs, instead of memorizing tree traversal algorithms. A policy student might present stakeholders, evidence, risks, implementation problems, and ethical tensions. AI can be a central part of all of these learning processes, but it cannot replace command of the work.

All Models Are Wrong, But Some Are Useful

None of this means the studio model is perfect. Architecture education has its own pathologies, and I have no interest in romanticizing them. Studio critique can reward confidence more than insight. It can privilege students who speak fluently, perform well in public, or know how to frame their work persuasively. Some students produce excellent work but struggle to explain it under pressure. Some are shy. Some are anxious. Some are still learning the language of the classroom. Some think visually, materially, or quietly before they think verbally. A live critique can reveal understanding, but it can also misread silence as weakness. There is also the problem of power. In a studio review, too much authority can sit in the hands of one instructor, one critic, or one charismatic jury member. Taste can disguise itself as rigor. Style can be mistaken for intelligence. A strong personality can dominate the room. Students may learn to satisfy the critic rather than develop the project. These are real problems. Studio teaching needs rubrics, reflection, documentation, multiple forms of feedback, and a variety of ways for students to show what they know. But this does not make the traditional model equally useful. In the age of AI, many of our old evaluation formats are either obsolete or much narrower than we like to admit. A pen-and-paper exam can still measure the ability to recall under pressure, fluency with a fixed procedure, or whether a student can perform a specific task without external tools. Sometimes that matters. But it is a small slice of knowledge, and in many fields it is not the slice we care about most. Great students can fail this kind of exam because it rewards a particular combination of memorization, speed, composure, and recitation. Those are not worthless skills, but they are a poor proxy for judgment. They are not the same as the ability to make something, revise it, explain it, test it against the world, and understand what changed along the way. The studio environment, at its best, already contains many of the qualities traditional education is trying to protect. It has memory, because students have to know their precedents. It has argument, because every design choice needs a reason. It has writing, because projects need framing. It has technical skill, because ideas must survive contact with materials, code, drawings, models, users, and constraints. It has evaluation, because the work is examined again and again. It has rigor, but the rigor is distributed across conversation, presentation, pin-up, review, revision, and demonstration. It is less tidy than an exam, harder to standardize, and therefore more demanding for the instructor. But it is closer to the way serious work actually happens.

Studio as a Habit

At the end of the year, in the same course for which I wrote that AI policy, I received the student evaluations. Two negative comments stayed with me. One student complained that the course was too "design studio heavy." Another wrote that it focused too much on presentations and public speaking in class. I read those comments and thought: good. Not because the students were wrong to feel the pressure. They were naming the pressure exactly. The course did ask them to stand up, present, explain, revise, defend, and return to the work. It asked them to be more than submitters of finished files. It asked them to become responsible for what they made. Even in a course that was not officially a design studio, those studio habits made students more engaged, more active, and more exposed to one another's thinking. Their projects became more exciting, I think, for them, and definitely for me. AI does not doom academia. It simply forces us to stop pretending that submission is the same as learning. The university is not finished because chatbots can write essays. It is in trouble only if it believes essays, exams, and finished files were ever the whole point. The point is judgment, agency, responsibility, and ownership. AI still cannot do these for you.

carouselItems.alt: Architecture studio workspace with students and project materials
